literature.journal_name: International journal of obesity and related metabolic disorders : journal of the International Association for the Study of Obesity

literature.title: No evidence for an association between genetic polymorphisms of beta(2)- and beta(3)-adrenergic receptor genes with body mass index in Aymara natives from Chile.
literature.abstract: We assessed the association between Gln27Glu and Trp64Arg genetic polymorphisms of the beta(2) (ADRB2) and beta(3) (ADRB3) adrenergic receptor genes with body mass index and other cardiovascular risk factors.

literature.title: A polymorphism in the alpha2a-adrenoceptor gene and endurance athlete status.
literature.abstract: In a case control study, we examined the allelic frequencies and genotype distributions of two restricted fragment length polymorphisms (RFLP) in the alpha-2A-adrenoceptor gene (ADRA2A) and beta-2-adrenoceptor gene (ADRB2) among elite endurance athletes (EEA) and sedentary controls (SC).
literature.journal_name: Medicine and science in sports and exercise

literature.title: Population frequency, mutation linkage and analytical methodology for the Arg16Gly, Gln27Glu and Thr164Ile polymorphisms in the beta2-adrenergic receptor among Turks.
literature.abstract: Inherited polymorphisms of codons 16, 27, and 164 of the beta2-adrenergic receptor (B2AR) gene may result in significantly changed functions of this receptor. The aim of the present study was to investigate the frequencies of the main mutations of the B2AR gene in Turks.

literature.title: Linkage analysis of glucocorticoid and beta2-adrenergic receptor genes with blood pressure and body mass index.
literature.abstract: Glucocorticoids and catecholamines exert important effects on cardiovascular physiology and metabolism. Variants of the glucocorticoid receptor gene (GRL) and the beta2-adrenergic receptor gene (ADRB2) have been associated with high blood pressure and obesity. These genes are close on human chromosome 5q31-5q32, and we undertook a linkage analysis of this region in 264 families from the general population in relation to systolic and diastolic blood pressure, body mass index, weight, height, and pulse rate. All family members were genotyped at four microsatellite loci (D5S207, D5S210, D5S519, and D5S119) located on chromosome 5q31-5q33.3. Using quantitative identity-by-descent sibling pair linkage analysis, we found that at no loci was genetic similarity associated with phenotypic similarity for systolic and diastolic blood pressure, body mass index, weight, height, or pulse rate. Although it is not possible to exclude the influence of specific combinations of certain GRL and ADRB2 polymorphisms, the absence of significant linkage in our population argues against a role for GRL or ADRB2 in physiological variation of blood pressure and body mass index.

results.table_description: Pharmacological characterization of LY2119620 a , Allosteric ternary complex model binding parameters for the interaction between LY2119620, iperoxo, and [ 3 H]-NMS at the human M 2 receptor. b , Operational model parameters for the functional allosteric interaction between iperoxo and LY2119620 at the human M 2 receptor. Estimated parameter values represent the mean ± SEM of three experiments performed in duplicate.
results.analysis: Extended Data Table 3 | Description: The table presents pharmacological characterization data for the allosteric interaction of LY2119620 with iperoxo and [3H]-NMS at the human M2 receptor. It includes binding parameters such as pKB, pK|, Logα, and Logα', with values derived from three experiments conducted in duplicate, indicating the reliability of the data. | Inference: The results suggest that LY2119620 exhibits specific binding characteristics and functional interactions at the human M2 receptor, which may have implications for its pharmacological profile.
results.keywords: LY2119620, allosteric interaction, M2 receptor, pharmacological characterization, binding parameters

results.table_description: Cox regression analysis of the NV risk at any given age in patients with DMD
results.analysis: Table 3 | Description: The table presents a Cox regression analysis assessing the risk of neuromuscular ventilation (NV) in patients with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD) based on various factors including genotype, age, ambulatory status, corticosteroid use, weight, and forced vital capacity (FVC). It reports statistical metrics such as beta coefficients (β), standard errors (SE), Wald statistics, p-values, hazard ratios (HR), and 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each variable. | Inference: The analysis indicates how different clinical and demographic factors influence the risk of NV in DMD patients, highlighting significant predictors of risk.
results.keywords: Cox regression, Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy, neuromuscular ventilation, genotype, age, ambulatory status, corticosteroid use, forced vital capacity

results.table_description: qPCR primers used in this study
results.analysis: TABLE 1 | Description: The table lists qPCR primers utilized in the study, detailing the specific sequences for forward and reverse primers, as well as probes for various genes. This information is crucial for understanding the molecular techniques employed to quantify gene expression. | Inference: The table supports the conclusion that specific primers and probes were designed for accurate amplification and detection of target genes in the study.

results.table_description: Ligand binding properties of mutant M 2 receptors Radioligand binding studies were carried out with membranes prepared from COS-7 cells transiently expressing the indicated mutant M 2 receptor constructs. ACh and iperoxo binding affinities (K i ) were determined in radioligand competition binding assays as indicated. ACh and iperoxo binding affinities (K i ) were determined in [ 3 H]-QNB competition binding assays for the N404Q 6.52 mutant, which did not bind [ 3 H]-NMS with sufficient affinity. Data are given as means ± SEM from two or three independent experiments, each performed in duplicate.

The results suggest that certain mutations may alter receptor functionality and ligand binding characteristics.
results.keywords: M2 receptor, ligand binding, radioligand competition, ACh, iperoxo, binding affinity, COS-7 cells

It includes five distinct metabolizer classifications: ultrarapid, normal, intermediate, poor, and indeterminate, along with examples of CYP2D6 diplotypes for each phenotype. | Inference: The table indicates that CYP2D6 phenotypes can be accurately predicted from specific genotypes, which may have implications for personalized medicine and drug metabolism.
results.keywords: CYP2D6, phenotype, genotype, metabolizer, diplotype

results.table_description: Information on microarray datasets.
results.analysis: TABLE 1 | Description: The table presents information on various microarray datasets related to liver conditions, specifically comparing control samples with those affected by alcoholic hepatitis and alcoholic steatosis. It lists four datasets along with their corresponding classifications for each condition. This structure allows for a quick overview of available data for further analysis. | Inference: The table indicates the existence of multiple datasets that can be utilized to study the effects of alcohol on liver health, highlighting the differentiation between control and disease states.
results.keywords: microarray, datasets, alcoholic hepatitis, alcoholic steatosis, liver disease

results.table_description: Demographic and anthropometric data and the results from the CPT in the combat athletes and control group.
results.analysis: Table 1 | Description: The table presents demographic and anthropometric data comparing combat athletes to a control group, including variables such as age, height, body mass, BMI, and results from two CPT assessments. It includes means and standard deviations for both groups, along with statistical comparisons indicated by U statistics, p-values, and effect sizes. | Inference: The data suggests significant differences in the anthropometric measures and CPT results between combat athletes and the comparison group, indicating the potential impact of combat training on these variables.
results.keywords: combat athletes, control group, demographic data, anthropometric measures, CPT, U statistics, p-value, effect size

results.table_description: Results of a multilevel regression examining DBP as a function of study group, pain threshold, and pain tolerance using the CPT and the different genotypes of ADRB2 polymorphisms rs1042713 and rs1042714.
results.analysis: Table 3 | Description: The table presents the results of a multilevel regression analysis examining diastolic blood pressure (DBP) in relation to study group, pain threshold, and pain tolerance, while considering the effects of ADRB2 polymorphisms rs1042713 and rs1042714. It includes estimates of effects (b), standard errors (SE), confidence intervals (Lower, Upper), degrees of freedom (df), t-values (t), and p-values (p) for various interactions and main effects. | Inference: The analysis suggests significant interactions between the study group and pain measurements with respect to DBP, influenced by specific ADRB2 genotypes, indicating a complex relationship between genetic factors and pain response.
results.keywords: diastolic blood pressure, multilevel regression, ADRB2 polymorphisms, pain threshold, pain tolerance, genotype interactions

results.analysis: The supplementary materials provide critical insights into the effects of diabetes mellitus (DM) on myocardial ischemia/reperfusion (I/R) injury in a rat model. The study methodology includes the use of streptozotocin (STZ) to induce diabetes, followed by coronary artery ligation to simulate I/R injury, allowing for a comprehensive evaluation of cardiac function through echocardiographic parameters. The findings indicate that DM exacerbates myocardial damage, as evidenced by increased infarct size and impaired left ventricular function, highlighting the clinical relevance of managing diabetes in patients at risk for cardiac events. This research underscores the need for targeted therapeutic strategies that address the interplay between metabolic disorders and cardiovascular health, potentially guiding future interventions in diabetic patients experiencing I/R injury.
results.keywords: streptozotocin, ischemia/reperfusion, echocardiography, left ventricular ejection fraction, myocardial infarct, coronary artery ligation, left ventricular internal diameter, cardiac function

results.analysis: The supplementary material provides critical insights into the effects of Clonorchis sinensis infection on bile duct morphology and macrophage activation. The histological analysis via HE staining reveals that infection leads to significant bile duct dilation, with a notable difference between Adrb2+/+ and Adrb2−/− mice, suggesting a role for the adrenergic signaling pathway in modulating the inflammatory response. The Western blot data indicate that IL-4 stimulation does not significantly alter PKA and AKT phosphorylation in bone marrow-derived macrophages (BMDM), which may imply a specific pathway of macrophage activation that is independent of these kinases. This information is pivotal for understanding the underlying mechanisms of host-parasite interactions and could inform therapeutic strategies targeting adrenergic receptors or macrophage polarization in related conditions. The comprehensive data presented in the supplementary materials enhances the robustness of the study's findings and provides a foundation for future translational research.
results.keywords: Clonorchis sinensis, bile duct dilation, Adrb2, IL-4, macrophage polarization, PKA, AKT, Western blot
results.description: (A)HE staining of the bile duct of normal andClonorchis sinensis–infected mice.C. sinensisinduces the dilatation of the bile duct in infected mice. Moreover, the dilation of the bile duct is more severe in infectedAdrb2+/+than that observed in infectedAdrb2−/−.(B)Western blot of thein vitroexperiment on BMDM. The stimulation of macrophages by IL-4 to induce AAMs does not induce a significant change in the phosphorylation of PKA and AKT compared with non-stimulated macrophages.(C)The experimental procedure used for thein vitroexperiment on macrophages.

results.analysis: The supplementary material provides critical insights into the role of adrenergic receptors, specifically α2A-AR and β2-AR, in the murine knee joint, particularly in the context of osteophyte formation following destabilization of the medial meniscus (DMM). The use of immunohistochemical staining allows for the precise localization and quantification of receptor expression in articular cartilage and synovium, which is essential for understanding the molecular mechanisms underlying joint remodeling and potential therapeutic targets. The data presented, including body weight and length measurements, alongside significant p-values, underscores the importance of β2-AR in modulating joint responses post-surgery, suggesting that targeting these receptors could have translational applications in joint health and disease management. The comprehensive methodology, including QQ plots for normal distribution checks, enhances the robustness of the findings, making them clinically relevant for future studies aimed at developing receptor-targeted therapies.
results.keywords: α2A-AR, β2-AR, immunohistochemistry, osteophyte formation, DMMB staining

results.analysis: The supplementary material provides critical data on gestational weight gain across different dietary groups, stratified by pregestational BMI, which is essential for understanding the impact of maternal nutrition on pregnancy outcomes. This data can inform clinical practices regarding dietary recommendations for pregnant individuals, emphasizing the importance of tailored interventions based on genetic and nutritional profiles. The methodology appears robust, utilizing a longitudinal study design over several years, which enhances the reliability of the findings and their applicability in clinical settings. Overall, this supplementary information enriches the primary research by offering comprehensive insights into the interplay between genetics, diet, and weight gain during pregnancy, with significant implications for maternal and fetal health.

results.analysis: The supplementary material provides critical insights into the expression of the β2-adrenergic receptor (ADRB2) in human hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) and breast cancer (BC) cell lines, highlighting its potential role in tumor metastasis. The study employs quantitative mRNA expression analysis across various cell lines, revealing that the Hep3B and MDA-MB-231 cell lines exhibit the highest ADRB2 expression, suggesting a possible link between β2-AR signaling and aggressive cancer phenotypes. Furthermore, the supplementary figures detail the effects of adrenergic agonists and antagonists, such as propranolol and dasatinib, on cell proliferation and migration, indicating potential therapeutic avenues for targeting β2-AR in cancer treatment. This research underscores the significance of adrenergic signaling pathways in cancer biology and their implications for developing novel therapeutic strategies aimed at inhibiting tumor progression and metastasis.
results.keywords: ADRB2, β2-adrenergic receptor, propranolol, dasatinib, mRNA expression, cell proliferation, cell migration, adrenergic signaling

results.analysis: The supplementary materials provide a robust methodological framework for identifying differentially expressed mRNAs (DE mRNAs) and immune-related genes (IRGs) in interspinous ligaments (ISL) and peripheral blood (PB) samples from patients with ankylosing spondylitis (AS) and kyphotic deformity (AS-KD). Utilizing the R package 'limma' for differential expression analysis, the study establishes stringent thresholds for significance, enhancing the reliability of the findings. The integration of bioinformatics tools such as KEGG, GO, and GSEA allows for a comprehensive understanding of the biological pathways involved, which may have therapeutic implications in targeting specific molecular mechanisms. The identification of 76 DE immune-related genes (DEIRGs) in ISL and 26 DEIRGs in PB suggests potential biomarkers for disease progression and therapeutic targets, highlighting the translational relevance of this research in developing targeted interventions for AS patients.
results.keywords: differential expression, immune-related genes, Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes, gene set enrichment analysis, beta-2 adrenergic receptor, protein-protein interaction, ceRNA sequencing, normalization

results.analysis: The supplementary material provides a systematic literature review that highlights the pharmacogenomic implications of various genotypes on beta-blocker dosing, particularly focusing on the CYP2D6 gene and its interactions with adrenergic receptor genes. This analysis is clinically relevant as it underscores the importance of personalized medicine in optimizing beta-blocker therapy based on genetic profiles, which can significantly influence drug metabolism and patient response. The methodology includes a comprehensive review of existing literature and the use of standardized nomenclature for genetic variants, which enhances the reliability of genotype-phenotype translations. The data presented in the supplementary tables offers a valuable resource for clinicians to interpret genetic test results and make informed decisions regarding beta-blocker prescriptions, thereby improving therapeutic outcomes. Furthermore, the emphasis on genetic testing options reflects a growing trend towards integrating pharmacogenomic data into clinical practice, which has the potential to reduce adverse drug reactions and enhance efficacy in cardiovascular therapies.
results.keywords: CYP2D6, beta-blockers, pharmacogenomics, genotype-phenotype translation, genetic testing, adrenergic receptors, drug metabolism

results.analysis: The supplementary material provides critical insights into the impact of sleep deprivation (SD) on ovarian function, particularly the loss of primordial follicles in a mouse model. The study employs a combination of methodologies, including body weight measurements, serum hormone level assessments, and advanced RNA sequencing techniques, to elucidate the molecular mechanisms underlying follicular development and loss. Notably, the identification of KITL as a differentially expressed gene highlights its potential role as a therapeutic target for preserving ovarian function under stress conditions. The findings underscore the importance of understanding the biological pathways affected by SD, such as Wnt and PI3K-AKT signaling, which may have translational applications in reproductive health and fertility preservation strategies. Overall, the comprehensive data presented in the supplementary materials enhances the understanding of ovarian biology and the effects of environmental stressors on reproductive health.
results.keywords: AMH, KITL, RNA-Seq, Wnt signaling, PI3K-AKT signaling, folliculogenesis, estrogen signaling, cAMP signaling

results.analysis: The supplementary material provides critical insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying the differentiation of mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) from healthy donors (HD-MSCs) and osteoporotic patients (OP-MSCs) under different culture conditions. The inclusion of uncropped Western blot images allows for a comprehensive evaluation of protein expression levels, specifically focusing on phosphorylated and total forms of CREB1 and ERK1/2, as well as cell cycle regulators Cyclin A and Cyclin B1. The statistical analysis presented enhances the reliability of the findings, indicating significant differences in protein levels that may correlate with the osteogenic potential of MSCs. This data is pivotal for understanding the therapeutic implications of MSCs in regenerative medicine, particularly in enhancing bone formation and addressing osteoporosis. The methodological rigor, including the use of Sanger sequencing and RT-PCR for genetic analysis, further supports the translational potential of these findings in developing targeted therapies for bone-related conditions.
results.keywords: p-CREB1, t-CREB1, p-ERK1/2, t-ERK1/2, Cyclin A, Cyclin B1, Sanger sequencing, RT-PCR

results.analysis: The supplementary material provides detailed methodological insights into the purification and labeling of the M2 muscarinic receptor, which is critical for understanding its pharmacological properties. The use of biotin-NHS ester for receptor labeling and the subsequent binding studies with various ligands, including atropine and tiotropium, highlight the receptor's role in drug interactions and potential therapeutic targets. The radioligand binding assays conducted with COS-7 cells expressing mutant M2 receptor constructs allow for the assessment of binding affinities, which is essential for elucidating the receptor's functional dynamics and the impact of specific mutations on ligand binding. This data is significant for the development of targeted therapies that modulate cholinergic signaling pathways, with implications for drug design and personalized medicine approaches in pharmacotherapy.

results.analysis: The supplementary materials provide critical insights into the role of Protein Kinase A (PKA) in modulating β2-adrenergic receptor (B2AR) signaling and endocytosis. The data demonstrate that PKA inhibition leads to an increased number of B2AR tubules per endosome, suggesting a potential mechanism by which PKA influences receptor trafficking and signaling dynamics. The use of various pharmacological agents, such as latrunculin A (latA) and Dyngo-4A, allows for a nuanced understanding of the cytoskeletal and endocytic pathways involved in B2AR signaling. The findings have significant therapeutic implications, particularly in the context of designing drugs that target B2AR signaling pathways for enhanced efficacy in clinical settings. The comprehensive data presented in the figures support the hypothesis that spatial encoding of B2AR signaling can dictate cellular responses, which may inform future research on receptor-targeted therapies.
results.keywords: PKA, B2AR, cAMP, latrunculin A, Dyngo-4A, endocytosis, TUBB, ASRT

results.analysis: The supplementary material provides a detailed overview of the study's methodology, including the recruitment of patients with Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) and healthy controls, ensuring a robust clinical framework for the research. The use of quantitative real-time PCR for gene expression analysis in the hippocampus highlights the focus on molecular mechanisms underlying MDD, particularly in relation to neurobiological changes. The stringent criteria for patient selection and the absence of psychotropic medication prior to the study enhance the validity of the findings, allowing for clearer insights into the biological underpinnings of depression. This research has significant therapeutic implications, as it may identify novel biomarkers or molecular targets for intervention, potentially leading to improved treatment strategies for MDD. The comprehensive reporting of clinical data and methodological rigor underscores the study's contribution to the understanding of depression at a molecular level, paving the way for future translational applications in psychiatric care.
results.keywords: quantitative PCR, gene expression, hippocampus, psychotropic medications, biomarkers, MDD, Hamilton Rating Scale, cDNA synthesis

results.analysis: The supplementary material provides critical insights into the role of ADRB2 in pulmonary fibrosis, highlighting its decreased expression in fibrotic lung tissues and its potential as a therapeutic target. The study employs a combination of gene expression analysis from the GEO database and functional assays in fibroblast cell lines to elucidate the effects of ADRB2 modulation on extracellular matrix (ECM) production and fibroblast-to-myofibroblast differentiation. The findings suggest that enhancing ADRB2 activity, through agonists like epinephrine, can exacerbate profibrotic responses, indicating a complex role for adrenergic signaling in fibrosis. This research underscores the importance of ADRB2 as a molecular target for therapeutic interventions aimed at modulating fibrotic processes in lung tissue, with implications for developing strategies to mitigate fibrosis progression. The comprehensive data presented, including unedited gels and additional figures, support the robustness of the findings and their potential translational applications in clinical settings.
results.keywords: ADRB2, TGF-β1, fibroblast-to-myofibroblast differentiation, α-SMA, epinephrine, extracellular matrix, ICI-118, profibrotic markers
